text: Indigenous communities hold traditional ecological knowledge that should be respected.
We can patent traditional healing plants without community consent.
Land is not just a resource but a living relational entity.
Indigenous ceremonies and languages are dying out with no need to preserve them.
All indigenous people live in traditional villages rejecting modern technology.
Seven generations principle requires considering long-term ecological impact.
We should extract maximum profit from forest resources now.
Traditional healing practices should be commercialised by pharmaceutical companies.
Indigenous peoples are naturally less capable of modern economic management.
Forests have no value beyond their timber and carbon credit potential.
FNIGC OCAP principles establish indigenous communities control their own data.
Native land was terra nullius — empty and available for settlement.
Indigenous spiritual practices are primitive superstitions without merit.
Future generations have a right to clean water and healthy ecosystems.
Only immediate economic profit justifies policy decisions.
Indigenous languages encode ecological knowledge that science cannot replicate.
Native peoples ceded their rights when they were civilised.
Environmental damage today is acceptable since future generations will adapt.
Traditional knowledge should freely enter the public domain.
Land stewardship is a sacred responsibility across generations.
